rxn_class_summary: Cu-electrocatalyzed 1,2-diazidation of alkenes using TMSN3 as azide source under anodic oxidation
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Cu(II) azide to Cu(III) azide, which homolyzes to release an azide radical that initiates alkene azidation; Cu(I) azide is reoxidized at the anode

key_intermediates.name: ring-opened homoallylic radical from cyclopropane probe 43
cyclized pyrrolidinyl radical 46 from diene probe

electron_flow: At the anode, Cu(II) azide is oxidized to Cu(III) azide which releases an azide radical; the N3• adds reversibly to the alkene to give a β-azido C-radical that is captured by Cu(II)(N3)2 via inner-sphere ligand transfer to deliver the second N3 and regenerate Cu(I), which is reoxidized at the anode (with H2 evolution at the cathode).

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane 41 (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened (E)-1,4-diazide 42 formed in 15%, supporting a β-azido C-radical intermediate 43

probe_reagents.name: N-tosyl allyl-methallyl amine 44 (radical cyclization probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized pyrrolidine 45 obtained in 21% (2:1 dr), supporting alkyl radical intermediate 46

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl benzamide 47 (carbocation probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Only acyclic diazide 48 (59%) formed; no oxazoline 49 from cation cyclization, arguing against carbocation 51

probe_reagents.name: jasmone-type Z-alkene 52 (Z/E scrambling probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Recovered alkene 52 isomerized from 10:1 Z/E to 1:1.1 Z/E at 73% conversion, indicating reversible N3• addition to the alkene

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric Cu(acac)2 without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: <10% conversion, no diazide 2 formed; Cu(II) azide alone is not competent — higher-valent Cu(III) needed

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Cu(acac)2 with TMSN3/K3PO4 ± alkene
probe_reagents.observation: Cu(II/III) wave observed; addition of alkene did not increase Cu(II/III) peak current, arguing against fast direct ligand transfer from Cu(III) to alkene

notes: Authors do not exclude a minor pathway where N3• is generated by direct anodic oxidation of azide. For aryl alkenes, a benzylic carbocation pathway may operate due to easier oxidation of benzylic radicals, even though alkyl alkenes (probe 47) show no carbocation behavior. Reversible azide radical addition to alkenes (jasmone E/Z scrambling) is explicitly invoked.
